title: The Bootstrap and Consonance Functions
text: examples , mainly used nonparametric bootstrap methods. show example using parametric Bca bootstrap results yields. First, ’ll load data set function. Now, ’ll use function, set method “bcapar” parametric method. Now can look outputs.   can compare functions see well bootstrap approximations match  concludes demonstration bootstrap method approximate consonance functions.
code: data(diabetes, package = "bcaboot") X <- diabetes$x y <- scale(diabetes$y, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE) lm.model <- lm(y ~ X - 1) mu.hat <- lm.model$fitted.values sigma.hat <- stats::sd(lm.model$residuals) t0 <- summary(lm.model)$adj.r.squared y.star <- sapply(mu.hat, rnorm, n = 1000, sd = sigma.hat) tt <- apply(y.star, 1, function(y) summary(lm(y ~ X - 1))$adj.r.squared) b.star <- y.star %*% X df <- curve_boot(method = "bcapar", t0 = t0, tt = tt, bb = b.star) ggcurve(df[[1]], nullvalue = FALSE) ggcurve(df[[3]], nullvalue = FALSE) plot_compare(df[[1]], df[[3]])

title: Troubleshooting
text: package computing thousands interval estimates via iterations bootstrapping, requires lot computational power. Luckily, concurve supports parallelization, although disabled default users, use Windows, unable use . However, able use parallelization, can enable following script script detect number cores machine via parallel package use speed computations, especially bootstrapping. However, like speed computations unable use parallelization, can reduce number steps concurve functions, drastically reduce time takes complete operation. default, steps arguments set 10000. example, changed number steps 100, minimum needed plot function, process now much quicker. can evaluate using microbenchmark. use bench package mark() function. using parallelization, must also set memory argument FALSE. ’ll now enable parallelization setting mc.cores option detect max number cores available using detectCores(). mc.cores option argument almost curve functions concurve package use parallel computing. setting number iterations 100, utilizing parallelization doesn’t seem help much, setting number iterations 500000 using multiple cores, seems computational advantage.
code: library(parallel) options(mc.cores = 1) library(bench) library(parallel) options(mc.cores = 1) getOption("mc.cores", 1L) set.seed(1031) func1 <- mark(df1 <- curve_rev(   point = 1.61, LL = 0.997, UL = 2.59,   measure = "ratio", steps = 100 ), memory = FALSE)  func2 <- mark(df1 <- curve_rev(   point = 1.61, LL = 0.997, UL = 2.59,   measure = "ratio", steps = 500000 ), memory = FALSE) #> Warning: Some expressions had a GC in every iteration; so filtering is #> disabled. getOption("mc.cores", 1L) set.seed(1031) func3 <- mark(df1 <- curve_rev(   point = 1.61, LL = 0.997, UL = 2.59,   measure = "ratio", steps = 100 ), memory = FALSE)  func4 <- mark(df1 <- curve_rev(   point = 1.61, LL = 0.997, UL = 2.59,   measure = "ratio", steps = 500000 ), memory = FALSE) #> Warning: Some expressions had a GC in every iteration; so filtering is #> disabled. func1$median #> [1] 748µs func2$median #> [1] 1.35s func3$median #> [1] 788µs func4$median #> [1] 980ms

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate random data for two groups in two studies GroupAData <- runif(20, min = 0, max = 100) GroupAMean <- round(mean(GroupAData), digits = 2) GroupASD <- round(sd(GroupAData), digits = 2)  GroupBData <- runif(20, min = 0, max = 100) GroupBMean <- round(mean(GroupBData), digits = 2) GroupBSD <- round(sd(GroupBData), digits = 2)  GroupCData <- runif(20, min = 0, max = 100) GroupCMean <- round(mean(GroupCData), digits = 2) GroupCSD <- round(sd(GroupCData), digits = 2)  GroupDData <- runif(20, min = 0, max = 100) GroupDMean <- round(mean(GroupDData), digits = 2) GroupDSD <- round(sd(GroupDData), digits = 2)  # Combine the data  StudyName <- c("Study1", "Study2") MeanTreatment <- c(GroupAMean, GroupCMean) MeanControl <- c(GroupBMean, GroupDMean) SDTreatment <- c(GroupASD, GroupCSD) SDControl <- c(GroupBSD, GroupDSD) NTreatment <- c(20, 20) NControl <- c(20, 20)  metadf <- data.frame(   StudyName, MeanTreatment, MeanControl,   SDTreatment, SDControl, NTreatment, NControl )  # Use metafor to calculate the standardized mean difference  library(metafor)  dat <- escalc(   measure = "SMD", m1i = MeanTreatment, sd1i = SDTreatment,   n1i = NTreatment, m2i = MeanControl, sd2i = SDControl,   n2i = NControl, data = metadf )  # Pool the data using a particular method. Here "FE" is the fixed-effects model  res <- rma(yi, vi,   data = dat, slab = paste(StudyName, sep = ", "),   method = "FE", digits = 2 )  # Calculate the intervals using the metainterval function  metaf <- curve_meta(res) } # }

title: Construct Consonance Functions from Any CI-Producing Function — curve_wrap
text: function repeatedly calls ci_func different confidence levels construct full consonance function. ci_func argument must function : Takes confidence level (numeric 0 1) first argument Returns numeric vector exactly length 2: c(lower_bound, upper_bound) Common patterns ci_func: Linear models: function(level) confint.default(model, parm = "x", level = level) GLMs (profile): function(level) confint(model, parm = "x", level = level) t-tests: function(level) t.test(x, conf.level = level)$conf.int Correlations: function(level) cor.test(x, y, conf.level = level)$conf.int Proportions: function(level) prop.test(x, n, conf.level = level)$conf.int Survival: function(level) exp(confint(coxph_model, level = level)["var",])

title: Construct Consonance Functions from Any CI-Producing Function — curve_wrap
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Example 1: Linear model data(mtcars) model <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars) ci_func <- function(level) confint.default(model, parm = "wt", level = level) result <- curve_wrap(ci_func) ggcurve(result[[1]], type = "c")  # Example 2: t-test x <- rnorm(30, mean = 5) ci_func <- function(level) t.test(x, conf.level = level)$conf.int result <- curve_wrap(ci_func) ggcurve(result[[1]], type = "c", nullvalue = 0)  # Example 3: Correlation x <- rnorm(50) y <- x + rnorm(50) ci_func <- function(level) cor.test(x, y, conf.level = level)$conf.int result <- curve_wrap(ci_func)  # Example 4: GLM with profile likelihood CIs model <- glm(am ~ wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) ci_func <- function(level) confint(model, parm = "wt", level = level) result <- curve_wrap(ci_func)  # Example 5: Proportion test ci_func <- function(level) prop.test(45, 100, conf.level = level)$conf.int result <- curve_wrap(ci_func)  # Example 6: Custom function with additional arguments my_ci <- function(level, data, method) {   cor.test(data$x, data$y, method = method, conf.level = level)$conf.int } df <- data.frame(x = rnorm(50), y = rnorm(50)) ci_func <- function(level) my_ci(level, data = df, method = "spearman") result <- curve_wrap(ci_func) } # }

title: Plots Consonance, Surprisal, and Likelihood Functions — ggcurve
text: data dataframe produced one interval functions intervals stored. type Choose whether plot "consonance" function, "surprisal" function "likelihood". default option set "c". type must set quotes, example ggcurve (type = "s") ggcurve(type = "c"). options include "pd" consonance distribution function, "cd" consonance density function, "l1" relative likelihood, "l2" log-likelihood, "l3" likelihood "d" deviance function. measure Indicates whether object log transformation normal/default. default setting "default". measure set "ratio", take logarithmically transformed values convert back normal values dataframe. typically setting used binary outcomes measures risk ratios, hazard ratios, odds ratios. levels Indicates interval levels plotted function. default set 0.95 plot 95% interval consonance function, levels can plotted using c() function example, levels = c(0.50, 0.75, 0.95). nullvalue Indicates whether null value measure plotted. default, set NULL, meaning plotted vertical line. Changing numerical vector specify region line plotted area shaded. input must numerical vector, example c(-0.5, 0.5) single numerical vector 0 1. position Determines orientation P-value (consonance) function. default, set "pyramid", meaning p-value function stand right side , like pyramid. However, can also inverted via option "inverted". also change sequence y-axes match orientation.can set , ggcurve(type = "c", data = df, position = "inverted"). title custom title graph. default, set "Consonance Function". order set title, must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, title = "Custom Title"). subtitle custom subtitle graph. default, set "function contains consonance/confidence intervals every level P-values." order set subtitle, must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, subtitle = "Custom Subtitle"). xaxis custom x-axis title graph. default, set "Range Values. order set x-axis title, must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, xaxis = "Hazard Ratio"). yaxis1 custom y-axis title graph. default, set "Consonance Level". order set y-axis title, must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, yxis1= "Confidence Level"). yaxis2 custom y-axis title graph. default, set "Levels CI". order set y-axis title, must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, yxis2= "Confidence Level"). color Item allows user choose color points ribbons graph. default, set color = "#555555". inputs must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, color = "#333333"). fill Item allows user choose color ribbons graph. default, set fill = "#239a98". inputs must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, fill = "#333333").
